mnemonics.title: MONO = ONE
mnemonics.content: MONOalphabetic = ONE fixed substitution alphabet for the entire message. Every occurrence of a letter always maps to the same cipher letter.

mnemonics.title: ETAOIN SHRDLU
mnemonics.content: English letter frequency order: E, T, A, O, I, N, S, H, R, D, L, U. Memorize this sequence to perform frequency analysis on any monoalphabetic ciphertext.

mnemonics.title: 26! Big but Broken
mnemonics.content: 26 factorial keys sounds secure, but Frequency Analysis Makes it Fail (FAMF). Large keyspace without hiding statistics = false security.

mnemonics.title: THE-AND-IS Pattern
mnemonics.content: Look for 3-letter words (THE, AND), 2-letter words (IS, IT, OF), and 1-letter words (A, I) in ciphertext to speed up frequency analysis.

mnemonics.title: Digraph Detection: TH-HE-IN-ER
mnemonics.content: Top English digraphs: TH, HE, IN, ER. Finding these in ciphertext reveals two letter mappings at once, accelerating the attack.

keyPoints: A Monoalphabetic Cipher substitutes each letter using a single, fixed key (permutation).
The main weakness is preserving the frequency distribution, making it vulnerable to frequency analysis.
Caesar Cipher and Atbash Cipher are specific types of monoalphabetic ciphers.
Keyspace of 26! makes brute-force impractical, but frequency analysis bypasses brute-force entirely.
Led to the development of polyalphabetic ciphers to overcome frequency vulnerability.
commonMistakes: Confusing it with a Polyalphabetic cipher (like the Vigenère cipher), which uses multiple substitution alphabets.
Assuming it is secure because of the large keyspace (26!), ignoring frequency analysis vulnerability.
Forgetting that the same plaintext letter ALWAYS maps to the same ciphertext letter in monoalphabetic.
